sentences.text: Sliding down.
It was the beginning of a real change in our lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You would find signs in stores-- Jews not wanted here.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The way to Jerusalem does not lead through this store.
The way to Jerusalem does not lead through this restaurant.
It was-- there was a definite change.

At that point, I joined the BDJJ, which stood for the Bund Deutsch Judische Jugend, which of course proved to be an oxymoron because you were either German or Jewish, except in the eyes of the kids that, in my neighborhood, we were Germans first.
We were Germans by nationality.
We were Jews by religion.
And for a period of a year, I spent my life in the Bund and its activities.
I was 13 at the time, and I became totally immersed.
I-- for an entire year, I never wore a dress.
All my dresses hung in the closet.
I-- for an entire year, I wore nothing but navy skirts and white blouses, the colors of Israel.
At that point, we were an exact copy of the Hitler Youth.
The Hitler Youth, in troops of 20 kids-- something like that, 30-- would parade through the streets of Berlin.
All traffic would have to stop.
Whenever you saw the Nazi flag, people would have to stand at attention, and salute, and say, heil Hitler.
And the kids loved it.
I mean, here they had priority over the adult world.
They also-- the Hitler Youth, once you joined the Hitler Youth, you didn't have to go to school on Saturdays like the rest of us.
Teachers could no longer fail you-- give you a failing grade in any course.
You got into the movies for free.
You had all kinds of goodies.
And the BDJJ was an exact copy of the Hitler Youth.
We marched, believe it or not.
In 1935 we marched through the streets of Berlin with our blue and white flags with the Mogen David on it, and traffic didn't stop for us.
But we marched.
Most of the time, we would go into quiet neighborhoods or to outskirts of Berlin, and we did our marching there.
But we had song books, and instead of the Horst Wessel Lied, which was the-- the National Anthem of the Nazi party, we sang the Hatikvah.
And instead of 'Jewish blood will spurt,' [?
Juden Blud Musst Spritzen, ?] which was the main battle cry of the Hitler Youth, we said, 'Tomorrow in Jerusalem.' I became very, very religious.
I went to temple every single Friday night, every Saturday, every holiday.
I went to Dr.
Joachim Prinz's temple, which was in West Berlin, close to my home.
And then I was afforded a great, great honor.
I was allowed to go to a meeting of all of the big shots of the BDJJ that had been brought to Berlin from all over Germany.

And before I passed out on the stage, after listening to three hours of speeches-- and I wasn't the only one that passed out-- I was listening to all the speeches and I thought to myself, my God, what they're really doing is they're copying Hitler.
And this is ridiculous.
We shouldn't be copying Hitler.
We should be doing our own thing.
And I resigned from the Bund the next day and never went back again.
But it was a very, very intense Jewish period in my life.
I started wearing dresses again and led a more normal life.
I don't think it had much effect.
The only effect that it had-- lifelong effect-- is that to this day, I cannot go into a temple.
I-- hate to even go to a bar mitzvah today.

I don't go for the holidays, because unfortunately it triggers something in my subconscious and I have two or three weeks' worth of nightmares where I wake up in a cold sweat in the middle of the night.
And I relive a lot of things that I have suppressed and that I don't wish to remember.
Other than that, the only thing that it really left me with is a lifelong [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] despising of fanaticism of any kind.
I don't like fanatics of the right or the left.
Well, the school experience, I had three very, very close friends in my class.
Two of them were not Jewish, and one of them was Jewish.
They were my closest friends.
And in 19-- well, 1936 was another turning point.
If you remember, the Olympics took place in Berlin in 1936.
And shortly before the foreign visitors, the journalists, the world press arrived in Berlin, Hitler made a speech and let it be known that he did not want-- he wanted all antisemitic signs removed.
He wanted all-- I'm talking about physical signs as well as psychological signs.
He wanted the German press to be very careful not to print anything antisemitic, and Berlin became 1926 rather than 1936.
And as a matter of fact, every school in Ger-- in Berlin was given two free tickets for the closing ceremonies, which were attended by Hitler.
And of all the people in the world, I got one of the two tickets.
And I attended the closing ceremonies of the Olympics, not far from Hitler, where all the German school children-- those wonderful Aryan school children-- were allowed to sit.
Of course, you-- everybody knows about Jesse Owens, so I don't have to mention anything about that.
You also know about the Jewish-- German Jewish.

Well, what-- what-- what came out of the Olympics was that, as soon as the last journalist had left the city, the antisemitism was put back into place in triple force. 13 new edicts came out the week after the last journalist had left Berlin.
And it was no longer permitted for a Jew to be married to a Gentile-- to a pure Aryan.
Hitler started sterilization.
It's one of the quirks of history, of fate, of nature, whatever you want to call it, that Hitler had the misfortune-- and that the world had the fortune-- that Hitler was born with black hair.
He had the blue eyes.

If-- there are papers have been found that, if Hitler had not had black hair, he had planned to sterilize even good Aryans after he had eliminated all of the Jews, obviously, after that final solution.
He had planned to sterilize all Germans who were not blond or blue eyed.
But he couldn't do it.
He couldn't get himself to do it, because he himself had black hair.
In '36, the teachers were told that they could no longer talk to us in class.
In I don't know whether it was the end of '36 or after Christmas vacation of 1937 that the classrooms all over Germany were rearranged.
And the-- until then, in school in Germany, you sat alphabetically.
If you were lucky, my name started with an F, so I sat between two other kids with either F or G, always in the second row, which was very nice.
The A's and the B's, C-D-E took up the first row.
We came back-- I think it was probably after Christmas vacations, but it could have been in the fall of '36 already-- that there were eight Jewish girls in my class.
I-- there was no co-education in Germany.
I went to the [SPEAKING GERMAN] in Berlin, [? lyceum ?] for girls.
About 300 girls, I imagine, in the school.
And the classes-- I think there were 8-- 28 kids in my class.
We had the same teacher throughout the entire secondary education-- the eight years.
We had a wonderful teacher who hated Hitler.
And the eight of us Jewish kids sat in a row, in the back row.
And after we were reassigned to these desks, the edict came out that we-- there was-- there was not to be any physical contact.
Touching a-- an Aryan hand would contaminate it forever.
Our best friends were no longer allowed to talk to us.
The teachers were no longer permitted to ask us questions.
They weren't even permitted to reprimand us.
There was to be no communication between Aryan and Jew.

Former best friends would see each other on the street and either the Aryan or the Jew would quietly walk to the other side of the street, to the other sidewalk, so that they would not be embarrassed.
It was only very, very brave Aryans who had the courage to openly talk to a Jew.
As I mentioned before, my father would get phone calls at night, and I'm sure they were made from public phone booths.
I don't think-- my father was not important enough for our phone to be bugged, but you didn't know.
Telephones were bugged all over the city.
Help-- domestics-- were paid to inform.
At that point, of course, we were no longer allowed to have multiple help.
We were only permitted to have one in help, and that one had to be an alte Schachtel-- an old, useless woman.
My mother found a jewel who hated Hitler.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were quite sure that she would never, never, never, under pain of death, say anything she had heard.
So we had substantial freedom inside the four walls of our own home.
In school, I was no longer allowed to talk to my former friends, only the Jewish girls in the back row.
We would-- the way the German school day was arranged, we had school for four hours, I believe, in the morning, with two recesses-- two or three.
Then we went home for the big meal-- we ate a lot-- and then we came back at 2:00, and we had classes from 2:00 to 4:00, so that we had six hours a day of instruction.
We went out to the schoolyard during the recesses, and we were no longer allowed to play with our friends.
We were no longer allowed any kind of contact.
And all of this culminated into one of the worst days of my life.
May I talk about the picnic?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- as in America, at the end of a school year, the school year in Germany ends before Easter.
In other words, March, April, depending on when Easter is.
The school year ends the week before Easter, you have a three week vacation, and then the new school year starts, beginning of April, something like that.
And it was customary then, as it is in America now, to have a class picnic.
And our teacher, this wonderful professor Neumann, who let it be known that she didn't approve of what Hitler was doing.
I believe it was in 1936 that Bernhard Rust became the Minister of Education.

Hitler had an incredible knack to always find the right-- I can't use any off language here-- but to-- Hitler always, from the bottom 2% of the barrel, he always fished out just the right man for every job.
He was absolutely brilliant at finding all these warped minds.
And Dr.

Bernhard Rust, his first speech was something that's well recorded, where he decided that formal education was a total waste of time and was totally unimportant, and it didn't count for anything in the German Reich.
And our teacher let it be known in class that she considered formal education still to be worth something.
She had several visits-- we had a class Nazi, Milla Millville, whose father was a Gauleiter.
That means he was a supervisor of all the-- the whole Nazi party was a pyramid, with Hitler the top.
And it was organized.
And it's very interesting.
People don't understand that Hitler used the period from 1923, the Putsch in Munich, when he went to jail for a year and dictated to Rudolf Hess.
Hitler spent from 1923 to 1933, he-- I don't think he slept much.
He organized the country in those 10 years.
And when he came to power-- when he engineered his election as chancellor and took over, became total dictator-- he had a spider's web in place all over the country.
He had informants in place.
He had that pyramid was built, and he had found most of these warped minds from the bottom 2% of the barrel.
Which could happen in America.
All you would need is a Senator McCarthy to come along during-- not in the 19-- not in 1950, when we had a great period of prosperity.
But let him come along in 1930, when we had a Great Depression in America.
And that is what Hitler found.
Fertile ground, and unemployment, and malcontents.
And Miller Millville's father had been a Gauleiter, right from the beginning, a supervisor of the whole district of all the littler Nazis.
And Frau Dr.
Neumann got several visits that we all knew about from the Gauleiter, Millville.
And nobody ever talked when Milla Millville was around, because I'm sure that she had several people arrested, Jews as well as pure Aryans.
If she didn't like them, she'd talk to her dad, and that was enough.
But anyway, the class picnic was always looked forward to with great anticipation.
A day of total freedom in the country, im Gruenewald.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got to the school the morning of the picnic and the bus was there and all the children piled into the bus, and the eight of us stood there very uncertainly, not knowing what we should do.
And we eventually, when all the other kids were sitting in the bus, we were eventually allowed to sit in the last rows, reminiscent of what was going on in America, in the South.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got to wherever we were going in the forest, outside of Berlin, and the kids all piled out.
And we came out slowly, and a little bit scared.
And they all chose sides for the first games, and we were not chosen.
And we, the eight of us, sat on a little clump-- a little hilly rise-- in the forest.
And we sat huddled together, watching the other kids have a great time.
And Frau Dr.
Neumann was famous all through school for serving up a fabulous lunch.
And when the time came for lunch to break, she blew a whistle and all the kids came running from all sides from their games.
And we were not allowed.
We were not given a piece of bread.
We were not given a sip of water.
We sat on that little clump all through the lunch period for an hour, while the girls joked and laughed and had a wonderful time and stuffed themselves.
And some of the Jewish girls started to cry.
And my girlfriend and I, who were the strong ones, kept poking them and saying, don't you dare.
And then came the afternoon and again, they were off on their games.
And we sat there.
And the way home was the same thing.
We sat in the back of the bus.
And by the time I got home-- by the time we were allowed to escape from that bus after the ride back to the school-- I ran home.
I never looked at any of my friends, any of the other kids.
I ran home and the flood of tears didn't come until I threw myself into my mother's lap.
And my mother put me to bed and-- this is ridiculous, but after all these years.
Helena, who was that marvel that my mother had found, made some hot chocolate for me.
And I slept for, like, 14 hours.
And the next morning, I woke up and Helena hadn't awakened me.
And that was-- I had to be awakened every single morning three times.
I liked to sleep, even then.
And I got up, and I was scared to death.
My God, I was late.
And my mother came into the room immediately, when she heard that I was up.
And Helena immediately came with my hot chocolate.
And I said, Mutti, Mutti, ich bin spaet.
It's late.
I'm-- I'm-- I'm-- What's going to happen to me?
And she said, don't worry.
You're not going.
You're not ever going to set foot in that school again.
Your father and I talked last night, and you will not go back to the [INAUDIBLE] schule.
And I never set foot inside the building again.
One of my best friends lived in the same building.
She was-- she had decent parents.
Her father was also a surgeon.
I have seen [INAUDIBLE] since then.
I saw her again.
She talked to me until the very last day that we left.
As a matter of fact, I'm quite sure that the day we moved out of the building, she must have come to give me a little goodbye present or to hug me.
I saw her again the first time I went back to Berlin in 1969, and I also have a picture of the building from that year.
And I finally went back to Berlin, on the one week's invitation, last year with my daughter.
And I saw [INAUDIBLE] again.
This is a girl who was born in the bed that she will die in.
She still lives in the same building, and it was incredible to find her still living there in 1969.
And-- But I never set foot in the building again.
My father made phone calls the next day.
He made appointments for the day after, and I went to a private Jewish school.
My father took me on interviews, and I was horrified at what I saw at first because they weren't real schools.
They were makeshift schools, and there wasn't real discipline.
And I was a very-- I was a real German girl.
I was-- my father was a Prussian to the core.
He looked like-- he-- all his life he stood ramrod straight like a Prussian officer should.
And he had the manners, the demeanor, the attitude.
And I was brought up very strictly, accordingly.
If I didn't finish what was on my plate, I got it for the next meal and I got nothing else until I'd eaten that.
And I enjoyed the discipline.
The first four years in German school, you sit at your slanted desk with your hands on the desk, with your thumbs underneath, and you don't move.
And if you move, your teacher comes around and raps your knuckles with a wooden ruler.
And my knuckles were never rapped.

And strangely enough, the German children with this incredible discipline-- I mean for six-year-olds, for 10-year-olds, no matter what-- loved school, much more so than the American kids who have this totally permissive atmosphere.
We couldn't wait to go to school.
I couldn't wait for vacations to be over and to go back.

I saw these makeshift Jewish schools, where kids were sitting on the floor, where they were sitting in circles, where there was no blackboard, there was no gym, there was no-- I-- I-- I was horrified.
And then, probably one of the last appointments of the day, we pulled up in front of this incredible villa, and I took one look at it.
And we walked around it because we were a little early, because we had left the last appointment in horror early-- we didn't even ask for a tour of this makeshift thing.
My father had already decided he was going to get tutors and have me tutored at home.
And we drove up in front of this villa im [?
Roseneck, ?] and I looked at this thing and I saw that in back there was a full athletic field.
And I literally-- I saw the French doors, going tall, French, glass doors-- and I literally thought I'd died and gone to heaven.
And we had our appointment.
And I took one look at the principal of the school, Frau Doctor Goldschmidt, and I had a crush on her, as did every single one of the 300 or so kids that went to her school.
We all adored this woman.
She was the most extraordinary educator that the world ever created.
And I would like to talk about the Goldschmidt Schule in detail, if I may.
She had, as every other Jewish teacher, been fired in 1935 when the Nuremberg Laws came out.
But before 1935, she had taken a sabbatical in England-- probably in '33, '32, '33-- and she had taught in England.
And when she was fired in early-- in '35-- she immediately went to Lo-- to Cambridge and she talked to some of her friends.
She had friends there.
And she said, I am going to start a private, Jewish school for extra bright Jewish Berlin children.
I am starting the school.
My husband is already negotiating for the building.
I've found the most wonderful building.
I want to become an adjunct of Cambridge.
And she talked them into becoming the Cambridge adjunct for Germany.
Graduating from the Goldschmidt Schule in Berlin meant entering-- the matrix of the Goldschmidt Schule was the entrance exam to Cambridge, which was an enormous achievement.
The school itself was a revolving door.
Every day, a child didn't appear.
Parents would either call and say, so and so is not coming back tomorrow, or the child just wouldn't come.
And if you called the apartment, the phone number, the house, whatever, there was no answer.
And you knew that someone else had either been arrested or hopefully had escaped during the night.
Or parents had a legal quota number and got out with their children.
And every day, somewhere in the school, a child or two did not appear.
That's why I say it was a revolving door.
Frau Dr.
Goldschmidt took one look at me and I was accepted.
I had to get papers from the [INAUDIBLE] schule that I had finished the term, which Frau Dr.
Neumann gladly gave my parents.
And I got straight A's, which she saw to whether I deserved them or not.
And I started the Goldschmidt schule.
Yes.
